Quick Facts
  • Lauryn DeLuca is a Paralympian and advocate for inclusivity in sports
  • She began fencing at nine, transitioned to wheelchair fencing in 2013, and became a five-time national champion
  • The youngest fencer at the Rio 2016 Paralympic Games, she was also the first wheelchair fencer in the NCAA while at Ohio State University
  • Graduating magna cum laude in 2022 with a degree in Child and Youth Studies and a minor in Disability Studies, Lauryn has worked nationwide in adaptive sports, including a fellowship with LA28, shaping accessibility and DEIB initiatives
  • She serves on the USA Fencing Board of Directors and the Team USA Athletes’ Commission and led efforts to bring parafencing to the NCAA championships stage in 2024
  • Now back in Ohio, DeLuca enjoys life with her husband, Brian Greve

USA Hockey

HEIGHT 5'4"  WEIGHT 140 lbs

Natalie DeRatt was born in Sheffield, England on June 7, 1988. The native of Asheville, North Carolina attended the University of North Carolina at Asheville, where she obtained a Bachelor's of Art degrees in english and mass communications. DeRatt played field hockey and netball through middle and high school, but she ran track at UNCA (everything from the 60m-400m.) She also competed in two track Olympic Trials ('08, '12) for team Great Britain.

She was introduced to the sport of bobsled by her college track coach, Brad DeWeese, who became the U.S. Olympic Committee strength and conditioning coach in Lake Placid after she graduated. Brad's intern and DeRatt's fellow track teammate at UNCA, Keith Scruggs, then encouraged her to try the combine in 2014.

FUN FACTS

- She has a massive St. Bernard named Carmen, who she deems to be the best pup ever.
- She is the marketing coordinator for Eagles Nest Outfitters. (Go getchu a hammock!).
- She makes really good chili, which according to her is funny because she's English - not a nation of chili eaters.

CAREER HIGHLIGHTS

  • 2015 World Championships in women's with pilot Jazmine Fenlator - 6th (2/15)
  • 2014-2015 World Cup with pilot Jazmine Fenlator - SILVER in Lake Placid (12/14); 4th in Calgary (12/14); 9th in Altenberg (1/15); 4th in La Plagne (1/15); 5th in Sochi (2/15)

GETTING TO KNOW DEWITT
Parents: Ron and Jackie DeWitt
Siblings: Krystian & Jacob
Pets: A pitbull named Lucy
Dream job: To be a speech pathologist in a school district
Favorite sport besides softball: Football
What it means to represent the United States: It is an honor to be able to wear Red, White and Blue! Being able to be a role model to all the little girls that watch softball is such an honor to me as well.
What she loves about softball: Being a part of a team and being able to do something that I love with some of my closest friends.
Who has been the biggest supporter of your career: My parents are my biggest supporters. When I was playing in California, I knew that after a bad game that they would be there to cheer me up. Going away to college I thought things would be different, but they are still here with me every step of the way. They are the people that are going to be there for me no matter what the situation is, every up and down, and I know I can count on them to be there.
Why she chose to attend Florida: I picked Florida my sophomore year of high school for a couple different reason. One, I fell in love with how pretty the campus is and two, I knew that the coaching staff here would make me feel as this was another place to call home. I also picked this school because they are the top ranked in the Speech Pathology College.

TRIPLE PLAY:
Three people I'd love to have dinner with: Florida Georgia Line, Jason Aldean and the Pretty Little Liars Cast
Three things I hate doing: Cleaning, shopping for a really long period of time and getting sunburned
Three things I love doing: Going to the beach, baking and hanging out with my friends
Three TV characters I wish were real so I could hang out with them: Joey, Phoebe and Chandler from Friends, and Aria from Pretty Little Liars
Three favorite TV Shows: Pretty Little Liars, The Walking Dead and Friends
Three favorite books: Gone Girl and any books by Nicholas Sparks
Three favorite websites: Facebook, Twitter and Pinterest

Quick Facts

  • Specialty: Forward

  • Daughter of John and Marilyn Decker

  • She has three brothers, Bryan, Ben and Brody

  • Began playing hockey at age three with her brothers

  • Enjoys listening to music, cooking, biking and playing cards

  • Founder of the Brianna Decker Endowment Fund for Girls Hockey 

  • Announced her retirement in March 2023

Biographical Information 

Brianna Decker is a former ice hockey forward known for her exceptional skills and contributions to women's hockey. She began playing hockey at age three with her brothers, and states “everyone one of us was a sore loser.” She credits her competitive spirit and determination to her childhood, growing up competing with her brothers and her parents instilling a strong work ethic in them. 

Decker played for the Wisconsin Badgers women's ice hockey team, where she earned the 2012 Patty Kazmaier Memorial Award, recognizing her as the top female collegiate hockey player in the U.S. 

Internationally, Decker has been a key player for the U.S. Women's National Team, earning a gold medal at the Olympic Winter Games PyeongChang 2018 and a silver at the Olympic Winter Games Sochi 2014. She also secured gold at the 2009 IIHF World Women's U18 Championship. 

Beyond her athletic achievements, Decker is committed to giving back to the community, actively participating in youth hockey clinics to inspire and mentor young players. She is also the founder of the Brianna Decker Endowment Fund for Girls Hockey, which provides grants to girls' hockey programs with the goal of increasing female youth participation in ice hockey by alleviating equipment costs.

Decker suffered a broken left fibula and torn ankle ligaments in the opening game of the Olympic Winter Games Beijing 2022, causing her to miss the remainder of the tournament. Having already served as an assistant coach for three Under-18 Women’s National teams, Decker made the transition to coaching full time, retiring from the ice herself. While she did consider returning to ice hockey after her recovery, she said her heart was ready to move on and stated “I want to make an impact like coaches did on me throughout my career.”

bio.fun_fact: He is a sergeant in the U.S. Army stationed in Lake Placid
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Position: Pilot
  • Maine high school state champion in alpine skiing
Biographical Information 

Frank Del Duca moved around growing up between Florida, South Carolina, and Maine. He became a Maine high school state champion in alpine skiing and walked on to the University of Maine track and field team, specializing in sprints and the long jump.

After college, Del Duca attended a bobsled combine and joined the U.S. bobsled team as a push athlete. After not making the 2018 U.S. Olympic Team, Del Duca moved from a push athlete to his pilot and would make his first U.S. Olympic team at the Olympic Winter Games Beijing 2022. 

At the 2025 IBSF World Championships in Lake Placid, New York, Del Duca piloted his team to a fourth-place finish in two-man and four-man bobsled events on his home track. 

Del Duca is also part of the U.S. Army's World Class Athlete Program (WCAP) and a sergeant in the U.S. Army stationed in Lake Placid.

Olympic Experience

    • Olympic Games Milan 2026, 4th (2-Man Competition - Men), 12th (4-Man Competition - Men)
